source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 4.7 Redirects and pipes (pp. 60-64)

questions.q: Which channel numbers correspond to the three standard streams?
questions.why: stdin is channel 0 (keyboard by default), stdout channel 1 and stderr channel 2 (both the screen by default).

questions.q: What is the difference between > and >> for output redirection?
options: > overwrites the file's contents; >> appends to them
> writes to the screen; >> writes to a file
questions.why: A single > overwrites the target (creating it if needed); >> appends. Both create the file if it does not exist.

questions.q: Write the output of 'date +%F' into the file date_file, replacing any existing contents.
questions.why: A single > overwrites date_file with the command's standard output.

questions.q: APPEND the output of 'date +%F' to the end of date_file without erasing it.
questions.why: >> appends the standard output to the file's existing content.

questions.q: Run 'ls -R /' and send only its ERROR output to the file errors_file (overwriting it).
questions.why: The error stream is channel 2, so '2>' redirects stderr; '2>>' would append instead.

questions.q: What does '2>&1' do, as in 'ls -R / >> log_file 2>&1'?
options: Redirects the error stream (2) to the same place as standard output (1)
Redirects standard output to the error stream
questions.why: 2>&1 points stderr at wherever stdout is currently going, so both streams land in the one file.

questions.q: Run 'ls -R /' and discard its error output entirely by sending it to the 'bottomless pit' /dev/null (appending).
questions.why: /dev/null discards anything written to it; redirecting stderr there silences a command's errors.

questions.q: What does a pipe (the | symbol) do?
options: Connects the standard output of one command to the standard input of the next
Redirects standard output to a file
Runs two commands in parallel with no connection
questions.why: A pipe feeds the left command's stdout into the right command's stdin; the commands after a pipe are typically filters (head, tail, sort, grep, wc).

questions.q: List '/' in long format and pipe the result through 'head' to see only the beginning.
questions.why: The pipe sends ls's output to head, which shows just the first lines.
